mapping.a_element: control parameter driving the spatial economy (e.g. transport cost / spatial scale)
mapping.b_element: control parameter of the gravity theory (horizon radius, coupling, Maxwell charge)

mapping.a_element: symmetric baseline population equilibrium along the line segment
sustain / bifurcation point where the symmetric state loses stability
mapping.b_element: threshold / minimal-horizon-radius where scalarized branches emerge and connect

mapping.a_element: emergent twin-city agglomeration as a new solution branch
mapping.b_element: scalarized solution branch that splits off

mapping.a_element: the several bifurcation behaviors / branch structure across models
mapping.b_element: the multi-branch structure and additional disconnected branch

mapping.a_element: transition between qualitatively different agglomeration patterns
mapping.b_element: transition from a single smooth branch to two disconnected branches

brief_justification: Both perform a bifurcation analysis of the equilibrium/solution branches of a nonlinear system as a control parameter is varied: paper A tracks a symmetry-breaking 'sustain point' where a uniform population state gives way to emergent twin-city branches, and paper B tracks critical horizon radii where scalarized black-hole solutions emerge, split, and reconnect into a multi-branch structure. The transferable machinery — parametrized solution branches with critical points marking branch emergence and splitting (spontaneous scalarization is itself a bifurcation off the bald solution) — maps role-for-role, so this is shared mechanism rather than mere shared 'branch/bifurcation' vocabulary.
